content: Let's cut to the chase because that is where beginners trip up most often when moving from simple tokens to a production-grade auth system using Rust and the jsonwebtoken crate. The single biggest mistake I see in interviews and code reviews is treating the Claims struct as if it were just a bag of loose data; you forget that this struct must implement the Serialize trait properly for your custom fields, but more importantly, you often ignore the algorithm selection entirely or pick an insecure default like HS256 without considering your threat model. If you are building a public-facing API where secrets might be leaked over wire, HS256 is fundamentally broken because anyone can intercept traffic and forge tokens; I always insist we switch to RS256 immediately once we have our signing keys configured via the jsonwebtoken crate's SecretKey and Verifier structures. Now let's talk about encoding and decoding; this is where the Claims struct really shines but also where people get confused by the difference between the raw token string and the parsed claims map. When you encode, you are essentially passing your Claims instance to the sign_with method along with your secret or key pair, which returns a signed JWT string that looks like a base64url encoded header followed by a dot and then the payload. The moment you decode that string using the verify method from jsonwebtoken with an appropriate Verifier, you get back exactly what you put in, but only if the signature is valid and the expiration claims haven't expired yet. I want to emphasize the algorithm selection because it dictates whether your system can handle token refresh scenarios gracefully; with RS256 you need two separate keys—one for signing new tokens and another for verifying them—whereas HS256 shares a single secret, which makes implementing a proper refresh flow significantly harder since you cannot distinguish between a revoked token and a legitimate one without additional state tracking. Regarding validation options, the jsonwebtoken crate provides a VerifierBuilder where you can chain multiple constraints like expiration time claims, issuer checks, audience checks, and even custom claim value assertions using your own predicates; this is critical because if you only verify the signature but ignore the algorithm header or the exp claim, you are vulnerable to clock skew attacks or replay attacks. For the refresh flow specifically, I recommend storing the public key of a separate signing service in your database or config store rather than hardcoding it, then using that key as part of your Verifier configuration so that when users request new access tokens via their refresh token endpoint, you can validate them without needing to re-sign anything. The Claims struct should always be designed with explicit fields for exp and iat (issued at) because jsonwebtoken expects these to be present in the decoded payload; if you omit them, your custom claims might pass but your entire flow will break later when trying to check expiration. Finally, never forget that signing keys are secrets; if you are using HS256, ensure those strings are encrypted at rest and rotated regularly, whereas with asymmetric algorithms like RS256, the private key must remain strictly offline and never exposed in logs or environment variables accessible to application code.
